message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Scaling ceiling of one-SA-per-person not bounded against project SA quota","location":"memo §Blast radius / §Scope and vocabulary (citizen = one SA per person)","type":"completeness","severity":"low","issue":"The model's central structural choice is 'one citizen per person = one SA.' GCP caps service accounts per project (default quota, increasable on request, but finite and console-displayed per project). For the current keyfile/solo posture this is a non-issue, but the memo frames the citizen tier as the durable shared capability layer that federation plugs into — and the explicit virtue of federation is no per-person SA. The asymmetry (citizen tier consumes one project-SA-quota slot per operator; federate tier consumes none) is a real platform-imposed scaling difference between the two tiers that the 'structural isomorphism' framing glosses over.","evidence":"Memo lines 17-18, 187-191 (one SA per person, forced). Google IAM quotas lists service-accounts-per-project as a per-project quota ('Varies depending on the project'; https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas). The memo's isomorphism claim (lines 33-38) names the identity layer as the only seam but does not surface this quota asymmetry as part of that seam.","suggested_fix":"Add one sentence to blast-radius or the isomorphism paragraph noting that citizen-tier identities consume a finite per-project SA quota whereas federates do not — a real (if distant) tier asymmetry beyond the resting-key difference already cited.","confidence":"medium"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the cited memo sections and checking the GCP SA quota claim.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

content.text:
Now let me examine the specific cited lines and the isomorphism framing. Let me look at the blast-radius and isomorphism sections more carefully, then verify the GCP SA-per-project quota claim.

The cited finding makes a factual claim about GCP capping service accounts per project. Let me verify this independently via web search.

input.query: GCP service accounts per project quota limit default 100

content.content:
Web search results for query: "GCP service accounts per project quota limit default 100"

Links: [{"title":"Project quota requests - Google Cloud Platform Console Help","url":"https://support.google.com/cloud/answer/6330231?hl=en"},{"title":"Quotas and limits | Identity and Access Management (IAM) | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas"},{"title":"Quotas & limits | Cloud Storage | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/storage/quotas"},{"title":"Quotas and usage limits | Resource Manager | Google Cloud Documentation","url":"https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/limits"},{"title":"GCP Quotas and Limits Explained: Rate Quotas, Allocation Quotas, and Increase Requests | CloudWebSchool","url":"https://cloudwebschool.com/docs/gcp/fundamentals/quotas-and-limits/"},{"title":"When workspace creation fails: Google project quotas – Terra Support","url":"https://support.terra.bio/hc/en-us/articles/6101030164507-When-workspace-creation-fails-Google-project-quotas"},{"title":"Google Cloud secrets engine | Vault | HashiCorp Developer","url":"https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/secrets/gcp"},{"title":"Usage limits and quotas | Admin console | Google for Developers","url":"https://developers.google.com/workspace/admin/alertcenter/guides/limits"}]

Based on the search results, I found the information you're looking for:

Each GCP project has a limit on the number of IAM Service Accounts, with a default quota limit of 100, including system-managed Service Accounts. You can request additional quota, and the quota increase is processed by humans, so it is best to request this additional quota in advance.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The factual claim is verified: GCP caps service accounts per project (default 100, increasable). Let me confirm the IAM quotas page directly to be thorough about the "per-project, varies/finite" framing.

input.prompt: What is the quota limit for service accounts per project? Is it a per-project finite quota? What is the default value and can it be increased?

content.content:
# Service Account Quotas in Google Cloud IAM

Based on the documentation, the service account quota situation is:

**Per-Project Status:** Yes, it is a per-project quota that varies by project.

**Default Value & Adjustability:** The document states that the "Number of service accounts" quota "Varies depending on the project. To view the quota for a project, enable the IAM API, then view your project's quotas in the Google Cloud console and search for **Service Account Count**."

The quota is adjustable. Since it appears in the "Quotas" section (not "Limits"), it can theoretically be increased by requesting an adjustment through the Google Cloud console, as noted in the introductory section: "If a quota is too low to meet your needs, you can use the Google Cloud console to request a quota adjustment for your project."

**Key Takeaway:** Google Cloud doesn't publicly specify a single default number—it's project-dependent, and you must check your specific project's quota settings in the console.

content.text:
Both claims are confirmed: service-accounts-per-project is a finite, per-project, increasable quota. Now let me assess the substance of the finding against the memo's actual framing.

The finding claims the memo's "structural isomorphism" glosses over a quota asymmetry: citizen tier consumes one SA-quota slot per operator, federate tier consumes none. Let me re-examine the relevant memo passages to judge whether this is a real gap and whether the finding's characterization is accurate.

Key memo passages:
- Lines 33-38 (isomorphism): The seam is explicitly identity-kind + principal handle + token mint. The capability layer is shared.
- Lines 195-198 (blast radius): "the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only ... federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself tier-blind."
- Line 19-20: "A federate holds no key." Identity is an external-IdP principal.

Now I'll evaluate. The memo's isomorphism claim is carefully scoped: it asserts the *capability layer* is identical modulo the identity layer's principal handle and token mint. A federate is an external IdP `principal://` subject — it does NOT create a GCP service account in the depot project at all. A citizen IS a depot-issued SA. So yes, there is a genuine resource-consumption asymmetry: citizens consume the project's SA-count quota; federates do not.

However, I need to judge whether this is a *real omission worth reporting* given the finding's own stated framing and the memo's existing treatment.

Let me weigh the considerations carefully:

1. **Is it factually true?** Yes — SA-per-project is a finite quota; citizens are SAs, federates are not. The asymmetry is real.

2. **Does the memo already cover it?** The memo's blast-radius section (lines 195-198) explicitly enumerates tier asymmetries: it names the resting-key difference as citizen-only, and explicitly notes what IS tier-blind (union-of-capabilities). It is careful to distinguish tier-specific from tier-blind properties. The SA-quota consumption asymmetry is a *third* citizen-only property not mentioned. So no, it is not currently covered.

3. **Is the isomorphism claim wrong?** No. The finding does not claim the isomorphism claim is false — and it isn't. The isomorphism claim is explicitly about the *capability layer*, and quota consumption is an identity-layer property. So the asymmetry actually sits *inside the seam the memo already identifies* (the identity layer differs by tier). The finding even acknowledges this: "names the identity layer as the only seam." The quota difference is a *consequence* of the already-named identity-layer difference, not a hidden additional seam.

This is the crux. The memo says: capability layer identical; identity layer differs (citizen = minted SA + key; federate = external principal, no mint). SA-quota consumption is a direct, obvious corollary of "citizen mints an SA, federate does not." The memo already states citizens mint SAs and federates don't. So the quota asymmetry is *entailed* by what's written; the finding asks to make one entailed corollary explicit.

Now the severity/value judgment. The finding itself rates this "low" severity and "medium" confidence, and concedes "For the current keyfile/solo posture this is a non-issue" and the relevance is "distant." The memo is explicitly about the *current keyfile tier* (line 12: "the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential tier"). For the actual posture this memo governs — solo operator, a handful of citizens (governor, director, retriever, maybe a few) — the 100-SA default quota is nowhere near binding. The federation tier where many operators would exist is explicitly future and out of heat scope.

Let me also check: does the finding mischaracterize the memo as claiming the tiers are isomorphic in resource consumption? Re-reading the finding's "issue": "the 'structural isomorphism' framing glosses over [the asymmetry]." But the memo's isomorphism framing is precisely scoped to the capability layer and explicitly carves out the identity layer as the seam — so the framing does NOT claim resource-consumption isomorphism. The finding slightly overstates what the framing claims.

This is a real-but-marginal observation. It is factually correct, not currently in the text, and the suggested fix (one sentence in blast-radius) is small and harmless. But it is a corollary of an already-stated difference, the memo already practices careful tier-asymmetry enumeration, and the finding's own framing ("distant," "non-issue for current posture," and slightly overstating the isomorphism claim) undercuts its value. 

My verdict: **partial**. The underlying fact is real and not currently stated, so it is not "false." But the finding's framing — that the isomorphism claim "glosses over" the asymmetry — is imprecise (the isomorphism claim is explicitly capability-layer-scoped and the quota difference is an entailed corollary of the identity-layer seam the memo already names). The better fix is not to add a new "tier asymmetry beyond resting-key" line that implies the framing was deficient, but to note the corollary where the memo already enumerates citizen-only properties (the blast-radius asymmetry bullet), and to scope it honestly as a non-binding-at-current-posture corollary.

Let me also verify there isn't already a mention of SA quota elsewhere in the memo that I missed. Scanning: the memo discusses SA tags, SA description sentinel, SA deletion tombstones — but nowhere mentions the SA *count* quota. Confirmed not present.

input.reasoning:
I independently verified both factual pillars. WebSearch + WebFetch of the IAM quotas page confirm "Number of service accounts" is a finite, per-project, project-varying, increase-on-request quota (default commonly cited as 100). I read the full memo. The asymmetry the finding names is real and not currently in the text: a citizen is a depot-minted SA (lines 16-18, 64) and therefore consumes one slot of the project's SA-count quota; a federate is an external-IdP principal:// subject holding no SA and no key (lines 19-20, 196), so it consumes none. The memo's blast-radius section already practices careful tier-asymmetry enumeration (lines 195-198: resting-key is citizen-only; union-of-capabilities is tier-blind) but does NOT mention SA-quota consumption as a third citizen-only property — so it is a genuine gap.

However, the finding's characterization is imprecise on one load-bearing point. It says the "structural isomorphism" framing "glosses over" this asymmetry. The isomorphism claim (lines 33-38) is explicitly scoped to the CAPABILITY layer ("the capability layer is identical across tiers modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies") and explicitly names the identity layer as "the one real seam" where citizen mints an SA and federate does not. SA-quota consumption is an identity-layer property — a direct corollary of the already-stated "citizen = mint SA, federate = no mint" difference, sitting INSIDE the seam the memo already identifies, not a hidden defect of the isomorphism framing. The finding even concedes the model names the identity layer as the seam, then faults the framing anyway. So the framing is not deficient; one entailed corollary is merely unstated.

Net: real, unstated fact (not false), but the stated fix is mis-aimed (it would imply the isomorphism framing was glossing over something, and proposes adding a "tier asymmetry beyond resting-key" line as if the asymmetry were a new seam). The finding's own ratings — low severity, medium confidence, "non-issue for current posture," "distant" — match my assessment that this is marginal. The memo explicitly governs the current keyfile/solo tier (line 12) where a handful of citizens sit far under the default quota.
input.refined_fix: Add the corollary where the memo already enumerates citizen-only properties — the blast-radius asymmetry bullet (lines 195-198) — rather than recasting it as a defect in the isomorphism framing. Suggested wording for that bullet: "A further citizen-only corollary of the identity-layer seam: each citizen consumes one slot of the project's finite per-project service-account quota (default ~100, increase-on-request), whereas a federate (an external IdP principal, no minted SA) consumes none. Non-binding at the current keyfile/solo posture's handful of citizens; relevant only if the citizen tier ever scaled to many operators before federation lands." Frame it as an entailed consequence of the already-stated 'citizen mints an SA, federate does not' difference, not as something the isomorphism claim missed (that claim is correctly scoped to the capability layer). Source: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/quotas (Service Account Count, per-project, varies/increasable).

content.text:
I've completed the adversarial verification of this finding.

Verdict: **partial** (low severity).

The factual core checks out — I confirmed via the GCP IAM quotas page that "Number of service accounts" is a finite, per-project, increase-on-request quota — and the asymmetry is genuinely absent from the memo. But the finding's framing is mis-aimed: it faults the "structural isomorphism" claim for "glossing over" the asymmetry, when that claim is explicitly scoped to the *capability* layer and already names the identity layer as the one real seam. The quota difference is an entailed corollary of the already-stated "citizen mints an SA, federate does not" difference, not a hidden defect of the framing. The refined fix relocates the note to the blast-radius bullet where the memo already enumerates citizen-only properties, and scopes it honestly as non-binding at the current solo posture.
